elements.sent-2411-001: Federated Learning (FL), as a mainstream privacy-preserving machine learning paradigm, offers promising solutions for privacy-critical domains such as healthcare and finance.
elements.sent-2411-002: Although extensive efforts have been dedicated from both academia and industry to improve the vanilla FL, little work focuses on the data pricing mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-003: In contrast to the straightforward in/post-training pricing techniques, we study a more difficult problem of pre-training pricing without direct information from the learning process.
elements.sent-2411-004: We propose \projecttitle that integrates a two-stage, auction-based pricing mechanism with a security protocol to address the utility-privacy conflict.
elements.sent-2411-005: Through comprehensive experiments, we show that the client selection according to \projecttitle can achieve more than 10\% higher accuracy in subsequent FL training compared to state-of-the-art methods.
elements.sent-2411-006: In addition, it outperforms the in-training baseline with more than 2\% accuracy increase and 3$\times$ run-time speedup.
elements.sent-2411-007: Federated Learning (FL) provides a privacy-preserving paradigm without exposing private data for learning machine learning (ML) models~\cite{deng2021auction,liu2020fedcoin,nagalapatti2021game .}
elements.sent-2411-008: It has found an increasing number of applications such as medical information systems~\cite{dayan2021federated , financial data analysis~\cite{basu-etal-2021-privacy , and cross-border corporate data integration~\cite{wan2023closed .}}}
elements.sent-2411-009: Research and industry efforts on FL primarily focus on improving the accuracy~\cite{li2020federated,wang2020federated , computation~\cite{lai2021oort,nishio2019client and communication performance~\cite{perazzone2022communication,ribero2020communication , as well as enhancing security and privacy~\cite{wang2022protect,bonawitz2017practical .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: However, little attention has been paid to incentivizing the participants to join FL, which is crucial because valuable data is not readily available for FL tasks without proper incentives.
elements.sent-2411-011: These data are often collected, cleaned, and pre-processed at considerable cost by third parties~\cite{schomm2013marketplaces,liu2014online .}
elements.sent-2411-012: A typical FL data market has three entities: data sellers (clients) who generate data and participate in FL tasks~\cite{spiekermann2015challenges , model buyers who purchase the model and FL data market (server) that coordinates between sellers and buyers~\cite{zheng2022fl .}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Existing incentive mechanisms mainly target the in/post-training pricing based on the accuracy improvement of FL training~\cite{zhan2020learning,tang2021incentive,zhan2021survey .}
elements.sent-2411-014: Unfortunately, the data providers (FL clients) cannot anticipate the reward before contributing their data and resources.
elements.sent-2411-015: Meanwhile, data buyers cannot evaluate the data quality before the actual training takes place.
elements.sent-2411-016: These are crucial because (1) anticipated rewards before training can greatly encourage the participation of clients, increasing client enrollment and the diversity of training data~\cite{zeng2022incentive,zhan2021survey .}
elements.sent-2411-017: For instance, based on our empirical survey~\footnote{The complete survey is shown in Appendix \ref{appendix:survey , 84.7\% of respondents believe that pre-training incentives increase their willingness to participate in FL.}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, 82.7\% of respondents prefer FL training with pre-training rewards over post-training rewards.
elements.sent-2411-019: Therefore, a framework for pre-training evaluation and pricing is indispensable for a sustainable FL data market.
elements.sent-2411-020: The medical research institution first publishes the task in the FL data market with the total payments.
elements.sent-2411-021: Then the task is forwarded to the corresponding healthcare providers to ask for their willingness to join.
elements.sent-2411-022: All healthcare providers who intend to participate in the training task negotiate a satisfactory reward for contributing their data.
elements.sent-2411-023: Hence, the data market collaborates with them to negotiate and establish reasonable pricing as an anticipated reward for subsequent FL training.
elements.sent-2411-024: To this end, the FL marketplace must have the ability to determine the price of each participating client before performing the actual FL training.
elements.sent-2411-025: The first challenge comes from limited information before training, i.e., we cannot use aggregated model accuracy as direct feedback for pricing and client selection.
elements.sent-2411-026: Instead, we need to peek into the statistics such as volume and categorical distributions, and analyze their correlations with model accuracy in a federated setting~\cite{deng2021auction .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Challenge 2: Pre-training pricing requires a consensus agreement between client and server.
elements.sent-2411-028: An optimal pricing system should effectively balance the needs of both the client and the server.
elements.sent-2411-029: From the client's perspective, pricing should not only reflect the intrinsic value of their data but also offer incentives that encourage them to share their data.
elements.sent-2411-030: On the server side, the total compensation should remain within budgetary limits, and the price set for each client should meet or exceed their expected value.
elements.sent-2411-031: Challenge 3: Pre-training pricing requires to solve the privacy-utility conflict.
elements.sent-2411-032: Although data volume and class distribution bring more insights for pre-training pricing, they also ask the clients to share distributional information regarding their private data.
elements.sent-2411-033: e.g., knowing the distribution of human activities would easily reveal individual habits~\cite{qian2020orchestrating,deshpande2004item , hence deviating from the original intention of FL to preserve privacy.}
elements.sent-2411-034: The price is determined through an auction that consists of a two-stage pricing mechanism, i.e., the initial price based on the statistical information and a contribution-proportional allocation strategy.
elements.sent-2411-035: The first-phase pricing determine the price of each client based on its statistical information and computed by a data value score function.
elements.sent-2411-036: To avoid client's sensitive information leakage, we design a privacy-preserving protocol to enable secured sharing of private distributions with the server.
elements.sent-2411-037: In addition, the second-phase pricing determine the actual payment of the selected clients through Budget-constrained Pricing Mechanism which is a consensus price between clients and server in terms of fairness and incentive.
elements.sent-2411-038: To the best of our knowledge, \projecttitle is the first framework that addresses the challenges for pre-training pricing in FL data markets, which would significantly motivate both data providers and buyers with a monetary incentive (see Table~\ref{tab:comparsion in the appendix for a comprehensive list of FL data sharing frameworks).}
elements.sent-2411-039: We propose an effective two-stage data pricing mechanism, in which the pricing of data explicitly reflects its value, the bidding costs of data providers, and the budget constraints of data buyers.
elements.sent-2411-040: We also design a privacy-preserving protocol to secure private information that could be potentially leaked during the bidding process.
elements.sent-2411-041: We have conducted a series of experiments to evaluate the performance of \projecttitle by selecting clients on three different datasets and in different levels of unbalanced data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-042: Our results show that \projecttitle achieves more than 10\% higher accuracy compared to state-of-the-art pre-training client selection baselines.
elements.sent-2411-043: In addition, it outperforms the in-training baseline with more than 2\% accuracy increase and 3$\times$ runtime speedup.
elements.sent-2411-044: This section outlines the related works on establishing an FL data marketplace.
elements.sent-2411-045: We focus on two crucial elements for constructing such a marketplace: Client Pricing and Incentive Mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-046: There are two main approaches to evaluating clients: model-based and data-based.
elements.sent-2411-047: For model-based approaches, FedCoin~\cite{liu2020fedcoin evaluates each client's contribution using the Shapley value.}
elements.sent-2411-048: However, computing the Shapley value is time-consuming because it requires calculating the value for all possible client combinations.
elements.sent-2411-049: Other model-based approaches assess each client based on their gradients \cite{gao2021fifl,zhang2021incentive,xu2021gradient or test performance \cite{li2024performance,deng2022improving,hu2022incentive .}}
elements.sent-2411-050: However, these methods introduce significant computational overhead and require in-training feedback.
elements.sent-2411-051: The data-based approach considers the quality of data to evaluate clients.
elements.sent-2411-052: For instance, AUCTION~\cite{deng2021auction assesses client contributions based on the data's mislabel rate and size, while Ren~\cite{Ren2024noniidauction compares the data class distribution with a reference distribution to evaluate client contributions.}}
elements.sent-2411-053: However, accurately assessing clients prior to training remains an area that requires further exploration.
elements.sent-2411-054: However, in real-world applications, information asymmetry between these parties often complicates the establishment of a standardized pricing model.
elements.sent-2411-055: To address this, auction-based mechanisms have been proposed, allowing clients and servers to negotiate prices.
elements.sent-2411-056: For instance, SARDA~\cite{sun2024socially introduces a social-aware iterative double auction mechanism to incentivize participation.}
elements.sent-2411-057: Similarly, Fair~\cite{deng2021fair employs a reverse auction model to attract high-quality clients while maintaining a manageable budget for the server.}
elements.sent-2411-058: Nevertheless, these mechanisms tend to rely on metrics such as model quantity or resource usage, often neglecting critical pre-training information related to user data.
elements.sent-2411-059: This pre-training information necessitates a more rigorous evaluation before training and requires the implementation of a security design that is both effective and privacy-preserving.
elements.section-2411-003: Data marketplace pricing Framework for federated learning
elements.sent-2411-060: In this section, we provide an overview of the framework with the designs of the two-phase pricing mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-061: Table~\ref{tab:notation in the appendix summarizes the notions of this paper.}
elements.sent-2411-062: Our framework consists of three parties: Data market (Server), Data sellers (Clients) and Data buyers.
elements.sent-2411-063: A buyer publishes an FL training task with a budget and the data market groups a set of clients to perform the training task.
elements.sent-2411-064: Finally, the data market returns a trained model to the buyer.
elements.sent-2411-065: Figure~\ref{fig:framework presents the overview of the \projecttitle pricing framework: \textcircled{1 seller first registers the FL task; \textcircled{2 the data market evaluates the data value of each client and returns an assessed price; \textcircled{3 clients make bids (quote) for contributing their data; \textcircled{4 the data market finalizes the price for the clients.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The pricing process consists of three steps: 1) evaluate data for each client by aggregating and getting global data distribution with a privacy-aware secret-sharing (PASS) protocol (see \S \ref{sec:system ); 2) send score function to the clients for computation; 3) receive the scores back at the server.}
elements.sent-2411-067: In principle, the clients with unseen classes and sufficient data volume have higher pricing and are regarded as high-quality clients.
elements.sent-2411-068: We assume that the participants would follow the protocols and refrain from modifying the data or sharing incorrect information with the server~\cite{zhang2021leakage,bonawitz2017practical since such malicious activities would be quickly detected.}
elements.sent-2411-069: On the other hand, the aggregation of individual class distribution would lead to privacy leakage as they often reveal sensitive information such as attribute percentage~\cite{zhang2021leakage , personal habits~\cite{deshpande2004item , sales record~\cite{hartmann2023distribution and financial status~\cite{chen2023protecting .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-070: We aim to design an integrated pricing and privacy-preserving framework to resolve the tension between data privacy and data pricing.
elements.sent-2411-071: We evaluate individual client data based on their contribution to the FL training, which is assessed by a score function.

elements.sent-2411-082: Each client $e$ has the amount of $N_e$ data.
elements.sent-2411-083: For each class $c$ from the same set of $C$ categories, the volume of $c$ at client $e$ is denoted as $n_e^c$.

elements.sent-2411-085: The score function on client $e$ is, \begin{equation} \label{eq:evaluation_function} u_e =\sum_{c=1}^{C} \theta_{c} \cdot \phi(n_{e}^{c}),\end{equation} where $\theta_{c}$ represents the unique coefficient for this category and $\phi(\cdot)$ models the relation between input data and model accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-086: The score function can be interpreted as the sum of the $\phi(\cdot)$ function, with the data volume of each category as the input, weighted by $\theta_{c}$ for the different categories.

elements.sent-2411-091: For the coefficient $\theta_c$, it can be calculated as: \begin{equation} \theta_c = 1 - n_s^c / N_s .\end{equation}.

elements.subsection-2411-003: Reaching a Consensus Price between Client and Server

elements.sent-2411-095: To satisfy clients requirements while ensuring the payments are within the given budget, we propose a second-phase pricing mechanism as follows.
elements.sent-2411-096: A client pool $\mathbb{E}$ consists of $E$ clients.
elements.sent-2411-097: The bidding procedures are designed to choose the most valuable clients within the budget constraint of $R$.
elements.sent-2411-098: We break down the bidding mechanism into two parts: winner selection and payment determination.
elements.sent-2411-099: Specifically, at the beginning of the bidding, client $e$ decides the bid $b_e$ according to the evaluation score and the potential cost of the task.
elements.sent-2411-100: The set of bids of $E$ clients is $\mathbb{B = \{b_e\}_{e \in \mathbb{E }$.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: The server selects the winning clients to join the task with the budget constraints $R$.
elements.sent-2411-102: And then decide the final payment $p_e$ for each winner $e$ with budget constraints.
elements.sent-2411-103: First, the server sorts clients by their score per bid and gets the list $\mathbb{V}$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Then, the server selects the winners in the order of $\mathbb{V}$.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Budget-constrained Pricing Mechanism} \label{algorithm1} Input: Candidate clients $\mathbb{E}$, Budget $R$, Bids $\mathbb{B} = \{b_e\}_{e \in \mathbb{E}}$ and scores $\mathbb{U} = \{u_e\}_{e \in \mathbb{E}}$ Output: Selected clients $\mathbb{S}_k$ and Payment $\mathbb{P}$ Winner Selection:\label{code:winner_begin} Sort according score per bid get $\mathbb{V}$: $\frac{u_1}{b_1}\geq...\geq \frac{u_e}{b_e} \geq...\geq \frac{u_E}{b_E}$ $\mathbb{S}_k\leftarrow \emptyset$; $U(\mathbb{S}_k)=\sum_{e\in \mathbb{S}_k}u_e$ \For{$\frac{u_e}{b_e}$ in $\mathbb{V}$}{ \If{$b_e > \frac{R}{2}\cdot \frac{u_e}{U(\mathbb{S}_k\cup \{e\})}$\label{code:budget_constraint}}{ break; } $\mathbb{S}_k\leftarrow \mathbb{S}_k \bigcup \{e\}$; \label{code:add_winner} }\label{code:winner_end} Payment Determination:\label{code:payment_begin} \For{$e$ in $\mathbb{S}_k$}{ $j\leftarrow 1$; $\mathbb{S}_{j-1}^{\prime}\leftarrow \emptyset$; $p_e\leftarrow 0$; $\mathbb{V}^{-e}$: $\frac{u_1}{b_1}\geq...\geq \frac{u_{E-1}}{b_{E-1}}$ \For{$\frac{u_j}{b_j}$ in $\mathbb{V}^{-e}$}{ \If{$b_j>\frac{R}{2}\cdot \frac{u_j}{U(\mathbb{S}_{j-1}^{\prime}\cup \{j\})}$} { break; } $\lambda_{e(j)}\leftarrow\frac{u_e\cdot b_j}{u_j}$; $\beta_{e(j)}\leftarrow \frac{R}{2}\cdot \frac{u_e}{U(\mathbb{S}_{j-1}^{\prime}\cup \{e\})}$; $p_{e(j)}^{\prime}=\min\{\lambda_{e(j)},\beta_{e(j)}\}$; $p_e=\max\{p_e,p_{e(j)}^{\prime}\}$; $\mathbb{S}_{j}^{\prime}\leftarrow\mathbb{S}_{j-1}^{\prime}\bigcup\{j\}$; $j\leftarrow j+1$ } $\lambda_{e(j)}\leftarrow\frac{u_e\cdot b_j}{u_j}$; $\beta_{e(j)}\leftarrow \frac{R}{2}\cdot \frac{u_e}{U(\mathbb{S}_{j-1}^{\prime}\cup \{e\})}$; $p_e\leftarrow\max\{p_e,\min\{\lambda_{e(j)},\beta_{e(j)}\}\}$;\label{code:payment_end} } $\mathbb{P} = \{p_e\}_{e\in \mathbb{S}_k}$ \Return{$\mathbb{S}_k$ and $\mathbb{P}$}

elements.sent-2411-211: In this section, we evaluate the impact of the client selection on the subsequent FL training accuracy by comparing \projecttitle to four state-of-the-art pre-training client selection methods.
elements.sent-2411-212: In addition, we compared \projecttitle with an in-training client selection algorithm, which can effectively use the training feedback.
elements.sent-2411-213: Experimental results show that, \projecttitle outperforms other state-of-the-art pre-training client selection methods in most cases.
elements.sent-2411-214: Even when compared to the in-training client selection algorithm, \projecttitle still achieves competitive accuracy with less runtime overhead.
elements.sent-2411-215: For IC, we utilize the CIFAR-10~\cite{krizhevsky2009learning and CINIC-10~\cite{darlow2018cinic datasets with a ResNet-56~\cite{he2016deep model.}}}
elements.sent-2411-216: For HAR, we employ the DEAP dataset~\cite{koelstra2011deap using peripheral physiological signals and train a three-layer CNN model on it.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Each client's data distribution is generated through a two-step process.
elements.sent-2411-218: Firstly, we adjust the global data distribution by randomly pruning data from different classes to achieve an unbalanced global distribution.
elements.sent-2411-219: In the second step, based on the different global data distributions, we allocate data for each class to each client following a Dirichlet distribution ($\alpha=0.5$) for simulating Non-i.i.d.. scenarios~\cite{hsu2019measuring,li2022federated,lin2020ensemble .}
elements.sent-2411-220: Appendix~\ref{sec_dataset shows the example of the generated dataset on CIFAR-10.}
elements.sent-2411-221: In summary, our evaluation encompasses three datasets, each tested under three selection ratios and six distributions (seven distributions for CINIC-10), resulting in a total of 57 test cases.
elements.sent-2411-222: We trained all FL tasks for 600 rounds, with an initial learning rate of 0.001 for CINIC-10 and 0.003 for CIFAR-10 and DEAP.
elements.sent-2411-223: To align with this focus, we selected four primary baseline methods: random selection (RS), quantity-based selection (QBS)~\cite{zeng2020fmore , DICE~\cite{saha2022data , and diversity-driven selection (DDS)~\cite{li2021sample , all of which are based on pre-training data evaluation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-224: It is worth noting that, while more recent works on model-based evaluation methods exist~\cite{li2023martfl,sun2022profit,sun2024socially , these methods require training feedback and can only be applied during the FL training phase.}
elements.sent-2411-225: Therefore, we included an in-training client selection method for reference.
elements.sent-2411-226: A brief description of each baseline method is provided in Appendix~\ref{baseline .}

elements.sent-2411-227: We evaluate the performance of \projecttitle on selecting clients by comparing the proposed algorithm with four pre-training client selection baselines.
elements.sent-2411-228: Figures~\ref{fig:cifar ,~\ref{fig:cinic and ~\ref{fig:deap show the final test accuracy of each baseline and \projecttitle.}}}
elements.sent-2411-229: It is observed that \projecttitle outperforms other baselines (RS, QBS, DICE, DDS) in a wide range of data distributions across three datasets, particularly when the distribution is highly unbalanced (e.g., D4, D5, D6).
elements.sent-2411-230: For CIFAR-10, \projecttitle achieves up to 40.78 $\%$, 44.78 $\%$, 17.52 $\%$ and 47.05 $\%$ (all on D6 in 10 out of 20 selection) higher accuracy when compared to RS, QBS, DICE and DDS, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-231: In terms of CINIC-10, compared to four baselines, \projecttitle has up to 28.10 $\%$ (on D6 in 10 out of 20 selection), 31.74 $\%$ (on D6 in 5 out of 20 selection), 5.44 $\%$ (on D5 in 15 out of 20 selection) and 7.27 $\%$ (on D6 in 15 out of 20 selection) higher accuracy, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-232: Regarding DEAP, Figure~\ref{fig:deap shows that \projecttitle achieves up to 25.67 $\%$ (on D4 in 5 out of 20 selection), 16.41 $\%$ (on D6 in 5 out of 20 selection), 8.63 $\%$ (on D6 in 5 out of 20 selection) and 29.21 $\%$ (on D6 in 5 out of 20 selection) higher accuracy than other baselines.}
elements.sent-2411-233: As the data distribution becomes increasingly unbalanced (e.g., D4, D5 and D6), \projecttitle exhibits significantly better accuracy than other baselines.
elements.sent-2411-234: This demonstrates \projecttitle's ability to mitigate the impact of global imbalance.
elements.sent-2411-235: Among all the baselines, DICE exhibits the best performance.
elements.sent-2411-236: Nonetheless, \projecttitle consistently outperforms DICE across three datasets and three different selection scenarios, with average accuracy improvements of 7.08 $\%$, 1.81 $\%$ and 3.53 $\%$ on CIFAR-10, CINIC-10 and DEAP respectively.
elements.sent-2411-237: Across all 57 tested distributions, \projecttitle achieves the highest accuracy in 44 distributions (77\%).
elements.sent-2411-238: For the most imbalanced distributions (D4, D5, and D6), \projecttitle achieves the highest accuracy in 22 out of 27 test distributions (81\%), highlighting its strength in handling imbalanced distributions.
elements.sent-2411-239: When the number of clients is scaled up to 100, \projecttitle can still achieve better performance compared to the baselines (the experimental results are detailed in Appendix~\ref{sec:large_clients ).}
elements.sent-2411-240: Figure~\ref{fig:my_convergence demonstrates that \projecttitle exhibits significantly faster convergence and attains a lower training loss, consistently outperforming other approaches.}
elements.sent-2411-241: In detail, \projecttitle achieves an target accuracy of 0.6 after only approximately 130 rounds, while RS, QBS, DICE and DDS require 550, 350, 170 and 380 rounds, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-242: This results in a speedup of 4.23 $\times$, 2.69 $\times$, 1.31 $\times$, and 2.92 $\times$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-243: We also observed similar conclusions in other datasets and distributions as well, where \projecttitle exhibits faster convergence compared to other baselines.
elements.sent-2411-244: We also observed a similar speedup in latency for \projecttitle in achieving the target accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-245: This outcome is expected, as \projecttitle conducts pre-training data evaluation once before the training, thereby adding no computational overhead during the FL training process.

elements.sent-2411-246: Specifically, we compare \projecttitle to DICE under the selection of 10 out of 20 clients using the global distribution D6 of CIFAR-10.
elements.sent-2411-247: As shown in Table~\ref{tab:selectedclients , both strategies select the same six clients from the client pool, namely, clients 2, 3, 7, 16, 17, and 19.}
elements.sent-2411-248: However, in the disjoint selected clients, we observe that the clients chosen by \projecttitle (i.e., 4, 8, 10, and 12) include data samples for all classes.
elements.sent-2411-249: In contrast, the clients selected by DICE (i.e., 1, 5, 13, and 18) missing some classes, i.e., classes 8, 9, and 10.
elements.sent-2411-250: Although DICE also takes into account the impact of both data quantity and class distributions, it suffers issues: 1) it ignores that the marginal utility of data volume is decreased; 2) It only locally considers the class distribution instead of the global distribution.
elements.sent-2411-251: These designs result in DICE favouring clients with larger data quantities and smaller local variances.
elements.sent-2411-252: In contrast, \projecttitle chooses clients with lower data quantities and global variance.

elements.sent-2411-253: In-training client selection methods are impractical for pre-training pricing since they assess clients' contributions during the FL training process.
elements.sent-2411-254: Nonetheless, we conducted a comparison on the accuracy between \projecttitle and a popular in-training client selection method, namely S-FedAvg~\cite{nagalapatti2021game .}
elements.sent-2411-255: S-FedAvg samples a subset of clients in each training round based on their Shapley values, which are calculated using their local accuracy.
elements.sent-2411-256: Figure~\ref{fig:sharpley_value shows the test accuracy and average runtime latency for both \projecttitle and S-FedAvg, with the selection of 5 clients out of 20 clients for all three datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-257: It is worth noting that S-FedAvg sampled 5 clients in every training round, whereas \projecttitle sampled 5 clients out of the 20 only once before the start of training.
elements.sent-2411-258: Overall, \projecttitle achieves an average of 66.12\%, 61.75\%, and 68.04\% final accuracy over all data distributions compared to an average of 66.67\%, 58.89\%, and 64.17\% on S-FedAvg.
elements.sent-2411-259: The results surprisingly demonstrate that \projecttitle can achieve comparable or even better accuracy performance than the in-training client selection approach.
elements.sent-2411-260: In terms of runtime overhead, \projecttitle outperforms S-FedAvg significantly.
elements.sent-2411-261: Completing 600 rounds of training, S-FedAvg takes 603 minutes, 1354 minutes, and 223 minutes, which is 5 $\times$, 2.5 $\times$, and 11 $\times$ longer than \projecttitle for the CIFAR-10, CINIC-10, and DEAP datasets, respectively.
elements.figure-2411-008: \subfloat[CIFAR-10]{ \includegraphics[width=.15\textwidth,height=0.13\textwidth]{./figuresTex/figs/sharpley_cifar10.pdf} \label{fig:sharpley_cifar10}} \subfloat[CINIC-10]{ \includegraphics[width=.15\textwidth,height=0.13\textwidth]{./figuresTex/figs/sharpley_cinic10.pdf} \label{fig:sharpley_cinic10}} \subfloat[DEAP]{ \includegraphics[width=.15\textwidth,height=0.13\textwidth]{./figuresTex/figs/sharpley_deap.pdf} \label{fig:sharpley_deap}} \caption{Comparison of performance between \projecttitle and S-FedAvg across three datasets} \label{fig:sharpley_value}
elements.sent-2411-262: In summary, compared to pre-training client selection baselines, \projecttitle achieves an average improvement of 10.18\% in accuracy across different datasets, data distributions, and selection modes.
elements.sent-2411-263: When compared to in-training client selection algorithms, \projecttitle still achieves an average improvement of over 2.1\% in accuracy and an average 3.16$\times$ speedup for per-round runtime latency.
elements.sent-2411-264: In this section, we discuss two challenges associated with deploying \projecttitle in real-world FL data markets.
elements.sent-2411-265: It is worth noting that we do not focus on these challenges in the design of our framework because simple modifications or existing solutions can effectively mitigate them.
elements.sent-2411-266: Moreover, these solutions can be seamlessly integrated into our framework, thereby minimizing the impact of these challenges on our core contributions.
elements.sent-2411-267: However, in real-world FL data markets, more advanced factors, such as market dynamics, may necessitate adaptive solutions for data pricing.
elements.sent-2411-268: For instance, rapid changes in client data or server budgets can influence optimal pricing outcomes.
elements.sent-2411-269: To address this, an adaptive re-launch mechanism can be incorporated into our framework, enabling the proposed bidding process to restart as needed to accommodate these variations.
elements.sent-2411-270: This protocol prevents the server from accessing sensitive client information.
elements.sent-2411-271: However, it does not guarantee protection against malicious clients who might provide false information or engage in fraudulent training to exploit rewards from the server.
elements.sent-2411-272: For instance, a common type of attack from malicious clients is the free-riding attack~\cite{lin2019free .}
elements.sent-2411-273: To address this, existing in-training free-riding attack detection techniques~\cite{Wang2023IOTJfree, Wang2022inferencedefence can be integrated into our framework to mitigate these vulnerabilities.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Based on the detection results, we can adjust the pre-training rewards to penalize malicious clients.
elements.sent-2411-275: In this paper, we propose \projecttitle, a privacy-preserved, pricing framework for pre-training client selection in federated learning.
elements.sent-2411-276: We design a truthful auction mechanism that is able to precisely determine the critical value and payment for the participating clients.
elements.sent-2411-277: Based on the aggregated class distribution, \projecttitle incorporates a secure data evaluation function and selects high-quality clients while meeting the budget requirements.
elements.sent-2411-278: Extensive experiments demonstrate that \projecttitle can evaluate the quality of local clients and select the best group of participants from the client pool, outperforming other baselines by a large margin.. \bibliographystyle{ACM-Reference-Format .}
elements.section-2411-009: Survey on the Impact of Pre-training Data Pricing on Participation Willingness in FL
elements.sent-2411-279: In this section, we provide our empirical survey on how data pricing influences users' willingness to participate in FL training.
elements.sent-2411-280: We first present our questionnaire and then provide a description of the participants.
elements.sent-2411-281: Based on the results collected, we then offer our analysis and conclusions, which highlight the significant importance of pre-training pricing in greatly enhancing the participation willingness of end users.
elements.sent-2411-282: First, we will provide some necessary background information on FL in case you are not familiar with it.
elements.sent-2411-283: Next, participants will be asked four multiple-choice questions about their thoughts on the pricing mechanism in FL.
elements.sent-2411-284: Please select the options that are most suitable for you.. \myparagraph{FL Basics.}

elements.sent-2411-285: Figure~\ref{fig:survey provides a typical training architecture for FL.}
elements.sent-2411-286: In FL training, your role is that of a client, holding your own data on personal devices such as smartphones.
elements.sent-2411-287: The server (e.g., companies) will distribute the training tasks to your smartphones and use your local resources to train a global model.
elements.sent-2411-288: Obviously, the training process will consume your personal resources, such as on-device computation and data communication.
elements.sent-2411-289: As compensation, the server will also offer rewards to the participating clients.
elements.sent-2411-290: FL is more privacy-preserving compared to traditional centralized training since no raw data is transmitted from your device to the server.. \myparagraph{Multiple-Choice Questions \question{.}}
elements.sent-2411-291: I have extensive knowledge and experience in the FL.
elements.sent-2411-292: I have some understanding of the FL.
elements.sent-2411-293: I have little to no understanding or experience in the FL.
elements.sent-2411-295: No, I don't want to try.
elements.sent-2411-296: I am not sure; I need more information to make my decision.
elements.sent-2411-297: I worry that the server might access my private data on my phone.
elements.sent-2411-298: The rewards are insufficient to cover the cost.
elements.sent-2411-300: I worry that the training will negatively impact my phone's performance.
elements.sent-2411-301: The server does not have corresponding costs for potential breaches, such as failing to compensate the client.
elements.sent-2411-302: A pre-training reward is evaluated and promised before training (e.g., you are promised \$10), and the final reward is adjusted after training based on the results.
elements.sent-2411-303: No pre-training rewards are provided, and the final reward is determined after training based on the results.
elements.sent-2411-304: A pre-training reward has a significant impact, changing my decision from not participating to participating.
elements.sent-2411-305: A pre-training reward has some impact, making participation more attractive.
elements.sent-2411-306: A pre-training reward has no impact.
elements.sent-2411-307: A pre-training reward has a negative impact, making me less likely to participate.
elements.sent-2411-308: To ensure a diverse range of participants, we deliberately targeted various groups, including students, teachers, researchers, and engineers.
elements.sent-2411-309: After 7 days of the survey, we collected a total of 53 questionnaires on five questions.
elements.sent-2411-310: Figure \ref{fig:qustionnaire displays pie charts of the options for these five questions.}
elements.sent-2411-311: Despite gaining a basic understanding of FL, many participants seemed hesitant to join the FL training.
elements.sent-2411-312: As shown in Figure~\ref{fig:Q2 , about 46.2\% of the respondents expressed a positive willingness to participate, while 26.9\% explicitly refused, and another 26.9\% wanted more information before making a decision.}
elements.sent-2411-313: This implies the importance of an incentive mechanism in FL to encourage more participation.
elements.sent-2411-314: Among all the concerns for joining FL training, privacy is the most important factor, with 44.2\% of respondents choosing this option.
elements.sent-2411-315: The second most significant concern is the rewards, accounting for 25\% of responses, as shown in Figure~\ref{fig:Q2 .}
elements.sent-2411-316: This highlights that privacy should be the top priority to secure more participants, and reasonable rewards serve as a good incentive.
elements.sent-2411-317: We then asked participants about their thoughts toward pre-training rewards compared to traditional post-training rewards.
elements.sent-2411-318: The results clearly show that a pre-training rewards mechanism is more effective in encouraging participation in FL.
elements.sent-2411-319: As shown in Figure \ref{fig:Q4 , 82.7\% of respondents find the pre-training pricing mechanism more attractive.}
elements.sent-2411-320: This conclusion is further supported by Figure \ref{fig:Q5 , where 84.6\% of respondents believe that pre-training pricing has a positive impact (either significant or some impact) on their decision to participate in FL.}
elements.sent-2411-321: These findings highlight that ensuring privacy and offering reasonable rewards are crucial to enhancing participation in FL training.
elements.sent-2411-322: Additionally, pre-training incentives are particularly effective in motivating participants.

elements.figure-2411-014: \caption{The PASS protocol} 0.45\textwidth{!}{ \label{tabel5} \begin{tabular}{p{8cm}} Set up: -- All clients are given the security parameter $r$ by the server $S$. Step 1: Client $e$: -- Honestly generate $R\leftarrow KA.param(r)$ and generate key pairs $(PK_e,SK_e)\leftarrow KA.gen(R)$. -- Send $PK_e$ to the server $S$. Server $S$: -- Receive public keys $PK_{e,e\in \mathbb{E}}$ from clients and broadcast ${(PK_e,e)}_{e\in \mathbb{E}}$ to every client. Step 2: Client $e$: -- Received the list $\{(PK_v,v)\}_{v\in \mathbb{E}}$ broadcast from the server $S$. -- For each client $v\in \mathbb{E}\setminus \{e\}$, generated random seed $s_{e,v}\leftarrow KA.agree(SK_e,PK_v)$. -- Based on $s_{e,v}$ generate $\mathbb{RV}_{e,v}\leftarrow \epsilon_{e,v}\cdot PRG(s_{e.v})$ using PRG, where $\epsilon_{e,v}=1$ if $e<v$ and $\epsilon_{e,v}=-1$ if $e>v$. -- Adding $\mathbb{N}_e$ to all $\mathbb{RV}_{e,v}$ yields the pseudo local data distribution $\mathbb{Y}_e\leftarrow \mathbb{N}_e+\sum_{v\in \mathbb{E}\setminus\{e\}}\mathbb{RV}_{e,v}$ and send it to the server. Server $S$: -- Receive pseudo local data distribution $Y_{e,e\in \mathbb{E}}$ from clients . -- Summing all $\mathbb{Y}_e$ to get the global distribution $\mathbb{N}_s\leftarrow\sum_{e\in \mathbb{E}}\mathbb{Y}_e $ and broadcast it to all clients. \end{tabular} } \label{tab:protocol}

elements.sent-2411-410: Figure~\ref{fig:dis_example shows the results of distributing the D1 global distribution to 20 clients.}
elements.sent-2411-411: A brief description of the baselines used in our experiments is provided below:.
elements.sent-2411-412: Random Selection (RS): a simple method that randomly selects a fixed number of clients.
elements.sent-2411-413: Quantity Based Selection (QBS)~\cite{zeng2020fmore : QBS selects a fixed number of clients only based on their data volumes in descending order.}
elements.sent-2411-414: DICE~\cite{saha2022data : DICE selects clients with the highest data quality scores, defined in terms of the data volume ratio and the standard deviation of data volume in different categories.}
elements.sent-2411-415: Diversity-driven Selection (DDS)~\cite{li2021sample : DDS selects a subset of clients based on two criteria, (i) Statistical homogeneity, which assesses the similarity between a client's distribution and a uniform distribution; ii) Content diversity, which measures the distance between clients on embedding vectors of their dataset.}
elements.sent-2411-416: The selection process favours clients with both high statistical homogeneity and content diversity.
elements.sent-2411-417: S-FedAvg~\cite{nagalapatti2021game : an in-training client selection method where the Sharply value of local model accuracy is introduced after each round of training.}
elements.sent-2411-418: In addition, a Shapley value-based Federated Averaging (S-FedAvg) algorithm is presented to select clients with high contributions to the FL task.
elements.sent-2411-419: The communication cost of the first part is $(1+(E-1)) L_k=E L_k$, where $L_k$ is the number of bits in the public key exchange.
elements.sent-2411-420: In the second part, each client uploads its pseudo data distribution $\mathbb{Y _e$ to the server, the size of $\mathbb{Y _e$ is $C\lceil \log_2 y_e^c \rceil$ ($y_e^c$ is the element of $\mathbb{Y _e$ and $\lceil \log_2 y_e^c \rceil$ is the minimum number of bits required for $y_e^c$).}}}
elements.sent-2411-422: Then the total communication cost of each client is $E L_k+C \lceil \log_2 y_e^c \rceil$, \myparagraph{Server Side Analysis On the server side, the two parts of communication cost are public key exchange and pseudo data distribution reception respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-423: For the public key exchange part, the communication cost of public key reception is $E L_k$ and the dispatch communication cost is $(E(E-1)) L_k$.
elements.sent-2411-424: The total communication cost of public key exchange is $E^2 L_k$.
elements.sent-2411-425: For pseudo data distribution receptiotn, he communication cost is $E C \lceil \log_2 y_e^c \rceil$.
elements.sent-2411-426: The communication cost of each client is $2ML_m$, $M$ is the total number of model parameters and $L_m$ is the number of bits in each parameter.
elements.sent-2411-427: The communication cost of the server is $2ML_m E$ due to the server transmitting the model parameters for each client.
elements.sent-2411-428: This communication cost will happen in each round of FL training.
elements.sent-2411-429: The number of parameters in the model is far greater than the class number and client number.
elements.sent-2411-430: Therefore the communication cost of FL training is far greater than PASS.
